page.content: In the ongoing analysis of staphyloma cases, our data set includes a comprehensive examination of various clinical metrics that influence patient outcomes. The table presented herein summarizes pertinent information regarding patients diagnosed with staphyloma, with a focus on critical parameters such as preoperative best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) and the axial length of the eye. Notably, the preoperative BCVA, measured using the Snellen chart, serves as a crucial indicator of visual function prior to surgical intervention.

page.content: In this context, the data reveals that a case number 1 corresponds to a 48-year-old female patient with a preoperative BCVA of 0.2, emphasizing the need for effective surgical strategies to address visual impairment. Additionally, the axial length, recorded in millimeters, provides insights into anatomical variations that may influence both surgical approach and postoperative outcomes. The presence of associated conditions, such as retinoschisis around the macular hole (MH), further complicates the clinical picture and highlights the importance of a multidisciplinary approach in the management of these patients. Collectively, these metrics underscore the intricate interplay between anatomical factors and visual acuity in the context of staphyloma management.
